title: Air-dry clay napkin ring set
subtitle: Set of four embossed clay rings formed around cardboard tube formers
excerpt: Four napkin rings formed by wrapping slab strips of air-dry clay around cardboard tube formers cut from a kitchen-roll tube. Each strip is embossed or incised while wet, then removed from the former at the leather-hard stage. PVA sealed. No kiln is required.
sourceNotes: Technique derived from first principles. Encyclopedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) pottery entry consulted for historical context on hand-forming methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness; each napkin ring is cut from a rolled slab strip
The drying stage at which clay holds its shape firmly enough to be removed from a former without deforming
The drying stage at which all moisture has left the clay and the piece is uniformly pale, ready for painting and sealing
Fully dried but unfired clay work that is fragile and must not be wetted before sealing
A creamy mixture of clay and water used to join the overlapping ends of the slab strip; dry clay ends will not bond without it
The technique of scratching cross-hatch marks into both clay surfaces and applying slip before pressing them together

content.text: Each napkin ring in this set is formed by wrapping a
slab
strip around a cardboard tube former, joining the ends with
slip
, and adding surface texture before the clay stiffens. No kiln is required. Each finished ring is approximately 45 mm in diameter and 40 mm tall, sized for standard fabric napkins.

materials.name: Cardboard kitchen-roll tube (cut into 40 mm rings)

content.text: Preparation

Cut a kitchen-roll cardboard tube into four equal rings, each 40 mm tall. Wrap each ring in a single layer of cling film; this prevents the clay sticking to the cardboard and allows the ring to be removed cleanly at the
leather-hard
stage. Prepare
score-and-slip
material: scratch a small amount of dried clay into a container and add water to produce a thick clay slip. Knead the working clay for two minutes before rolling.
Construction
Roll the clay to 4 mm thickness. Use guide rails to maintain consistent thickness across the entire sheet. A 4 mm wall gives enough strength without the rings becoming too heavy to be practical.

Cut the strips. Each strip should be 40 mm wide and long enough to travel around the circumference of the cardboard tube (approximately 140 mm for a 45 mm diameter tube) plus 10 mm for the overlap. Cut four identical strips using the fettling knife against a ruler.

Add surface decoration before wrapping. While the strip is still flat on the board, press a stamp, a section of textured fabric, or a seed pod into the outer face to create an impression. Incise a border line 5 mm from each long edge using the needle tool. Do not texture the final 15 mm of one end: this will be the underlapping join end and a smooth surface gives a cleaner join line.

Wrap the strip around the former. Lift the strip carefully and wrap it around the cling-filmed cardboard ring with the textured face outward. Bring the two ends together. Score the overlapping end with the needle tool and apply slip to both surfaces at the join, then press firmly together. Smooth the join with a damp finger. Hold for 30 seconds.

Check the top and bottom edges. Trim any excess clay from the upper and lower edges so they are level and flush with the cardboard former. Smooth the edges with a damp sponge. Stand each ring upright on the board.
Repeat for all four rings. Work on all four within the same session so the clay does not dry unevenly between pieces.

Remove the formers at the leather-hard stage. When the rings are firm enough to hold their shape (typically 12-18 hours), slide the cardboard former out from inside each ring. Peel away the cling film. Stand the rings upright to finish drying. If the former does not slide out freely, compress it slightly by squeezing the cardboard gently before pulling.
Drying
Stand the rings upright on the board after former removal and allow them to reach the
bone-dry
stage at room temperature (total drying time 36-2 days). The
greenware

rings are fragile at the join line; do not stack them until fully dry. Check the join line on each ring: if any gap has opened during drying, fill it with a thin line of undiluted PVA before painting.
Finishing

Paint all four rings with acrylic paint and allow to dry fully (at least two hours). Apply two coats of PVA diluted 50:50 with water to all surfaces, including the inside of each ring, allowing each coat to dry before applying the next. A glossy PVA coat enhances impressed details; a matte medium gives a more natural clay appearance.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof even after PVA sealing. These rings are not suitable as water vessels and should not be washed in a dishwasher or submerged in water. Wipe clean with a dry or barely damp cloth. Keep indoors. Do not use on damp or heavily starched napkins as sustained moisture will soften the clay over time.
Variations

Botanical impressions: press fern fronds, small leaves, or flat herb sprigs into the soft slab strip before wrapping. Peel away the plant material after pressing to leave a detailed botanical impression.
Wider cuff ring: cut the strips 60 mm wide instead of 40 mm for a taller, cuff-style ring that has more surface area for decoration.
Monogram rings: at the leather-hard stage, incise a different initial letter into each ring. Each family member gets their own personalised ring.

rows.cause: Insufficient slip at the join, or one surface had dried slightly before pressing
rows.fix: At leather-hard, apply fresh slip into the gap and press closed. At bone-dry, fill with undiluted PVA and allow to cure; sand flush if needed before painting

rows.cause: The clay was wrapped around the former while too stiff, causing the outer surface to crack under tension
rows.fix: Roll and wrap the strip immediately after cutting while the clay is still fully pliable. If cracks appear before removal from the former, dampen the outer surface very lightly with a sponge and smooth closed

rows.cause: Cling film was not applied, or had a gap
rows.fix: Squeeze the cardboard tube gently to reduce its diameter slightly, which breaks the suction. Ease the ring off slowly. If cling film was omitted entirely, moisten the cardboard exterior and wait 10 minutes for it to soften

rows.cause: The stamp was not pressed with sufficient pressure, or the clay had already started to stiffen
rows.fix: Stamp the strip immediately after rolling, before it has time to begin drying. Press the stamp with firm, even pressure and rock it slightly to ensure full contact
